section.title: Preparing to Cross the Jordan
section.sectionRationale: God's charge to Joshua now becomes action. Joshua issues his first orders as leader: the whole camp is to prepare provisions to cross the Jordan within three days, and the eastern tribes, who already have their land, are reminded of their pledge to fight alongside their brothers until all Israel has rest. The people answer with wholehearted obedience and their own echo of God's charge, be strong and courageous. It is one movement, the mobilizing of a united people for the promise ahead.
section.thesis: The rest God gives is a shared rest: the tribes already settled must not enjoy their inheritance while their brothers are still without theirs, and the united, obedient response of the people, echoing back the very words God spoke to Joshua, shows Israel taking up the promise together under God's word.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the order to prepare provisions to cross within three days, verses 10 to 11; the reminder to the eastern tribes of their pledge to help until all have rest, verses 12 to 15; and the people's wholehearted answer of obedience and courage, verses 16 to 18.

units.label: The order to prepare to cross
units.note: within three days you will cross the Jordan

verses.text: Then Joshua commanded the officers of the people:

“Go through the camp and tell the people, ‘Prepare your provisions, for within three days you will cross the Jordan to go in and take {{yarash|possession}} of the land that the LORD your God is giving you to possess.’”

units.label: The eastern tribes reminded of their pledge
units.note: until the LORD gives your brothers rest

verses.text: But to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, Joshua said,
“Remember what Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you when he said, ‘The LORD your God will give you {{menuchah|rest}}, and He will give you this land.’

Your wives, your young children, and your livestock may remain in the land that Moses gave you on this side of the Jordan. But all your mighty men of valor must be armed for battle to cross over ahead of your brothers and help them,

until the LORD gives them rest as He has done for you, and your brothers also possess the land that the LORD your God is giving them. Then you may return to the land of your {{nachalah|inheritance}} and take possession of that which Moses the servant of the LORD gave you on the east side of the Jordan.”

units.label: The people's wholehearted answer
units.note: all you command us we will do; be strong and courageous

verses.text: So they answered Joshua, “Everything you have commanded us we will do, and everywhere you send us we will go.
Just as we obeyed Moses in all things, so we will obey you. And may the LORD your God be with you, as He was with Moses.
Anyone who rebels against your order and does not obey your words, all that you command him, will be put to death. Above all, be {{chazaq|strong}} and courageous!”

yarash.gloss: To take possession of, to dispossess, to inherit. This is one of the key verbs of Joshua: Israel is to go in and take possession of the land the LORD is giving. It holds together two truths that run through the whole book, the land is God's gift, and yet it must be actively taken hold of; grace given is grace to be entered by faith and obedience.

menuchah.gloss: Rest, a settled resting place, security. The LORD's gift to His people is menuchah, a home where the wandering and warfare cease. In Joshua rest means secure settlement in the land; the New Testament shows this earthly rest to be a shadow of the deeper Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God in Christ, Hebrews 4:9.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, allotted possession, heritage. The land each tribe receives is its nachalah, a portion handed down by promise rather than seized by right. The eastern tribes already hold their inheritance east of the Jordan, yet they must help win their brothers' inheritance before returning to enjoy their own.

chazaq.gloss: To be strong, firm, resolute. The very command God pressed on Joshua, be strong, the people now speak back to him, be strong and courageous. Their obedience is not grudging but eager, taking up God's own word and returning it to their leader as encouragement.

basic.context: God has commissioned Joshua; now Joshua acts. His first order as leader is practical: get ready, because in three days we cross the Jordan into the land God is giving us. Then he turns to the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh, who had already received land on the east side of the Jordan. They had promised Moses they would send their fighting men across to help the other tribes conquer their land before settling down themselves. Joshua holds them to it: no one rests until everyone rests. The people respond with striking unity, promising full obedience and echoing God's own charge back to Joshua, be strong and courageous. The question underneath is: what does it look like for God's people to take up His promise together?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing how Israel mobilized. The specific arrangement with the eastern tribes belongs to that moment, and the harsh penalty for rebellion reflects Israel's covenant life under Moses' law, not a rule for the church. What endures is the pattern: God's people obey His word, act on His promise, and refuse to enjoy their own blessing while their brothers still lack theirs. The rest sought here also points beyond itself to a greater rest.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel is to go in and yarash, take possession of, the land God gives, a verb that holds grace and effort together. God's gift is menuchah, rest, a settled home. Each tribe's portion is its nachalah, its inheritance. And the people answer Joshua by echoing God's word to him: be chazaq, be strong.
basic.typology: The eastern tribes crossing over to fight for their brothers' rest before enjoying their own pictures the way God's people are bound together, not resting alone but laboring for one another's inheritance. The rest of the land points forward to the true rest in Christ. Joshua leading a united people foreshadows Christ gathering one people into one inheritance.
basic.christ: The rest Israel seeks in the land is a shadow of the rest Jesus gives: Come to Me, all you who are weary, and I will give you rest, Matthew 11:28. And the solidarity of the tribes, none resting while others lack, reflects the body of Christ, where members care for one another and no one is meant to arrive at the inheritance alone.
basic.god: God is the giver of rest and land; every step forward rests on what the LORD your God is giving you. He is the God who keeps His people together in one purpose, and who is worthy of the wholehearted obedience the people pledge. The whole scene assumes that God's promise is sure, so that preparation and courage are simply the fitting response to His faithfulness.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 32 records the original pledge of the eastern tribes to Moses. Deuteronomy 3 repeats Moses' charge to them. Hebrews 4 shows that Israel's rest in the land was a shadow of the greater rest in Christ. And Galatians 6 calls believers to bear one another's burdens, echoing the tribes' shared labor.
basic.oneStory: The promise of land moves from God's charge to Joshua into the united action of the whole people. Israel prepares to cross, the settled tribes take up their brothers' cause, and all pledge obedience. It is another step in God bringing His people into their inheritance, and the rest they seek points ahead to the lasting rest Jesus gives.

context.literary: This section turns the commissioning speech of verses 1 to 9 into Joshua's first commands, showing the charge already bearing fruit in action. It is structured in three exchanges: Joshua to the officers, Joshua to the eastern tribes, and the people back to Joshua. The people's closing words, be strong and courageous, deliberately echo God's fourfold command to Joshua, binding the section to what came before and showing the whole nation now sharing the leader's charge.
context.historical: The tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh had asked Moses for the good grazing land east of the Jordan, and Moses granted it on condition that their warriors cross over and fight until the rest of Israel was settled, Numbers 32. The Jordan at the spring flood, near the time of Passover, would be at full, dangerous flood stage, Joshua 3:15, making the three-day preparation and the coming crossing all the more striking. Provisions had to be gathered because the daily manna would soon cease once they entered the land, Joshua 5:12.
context.canonical: The passage continues the fulfillment of the land promise begun in verses 1 to 9 and rooted in the covenant with Abraham. Its theme of rest connects backward to the Sabbath of creation and the promised rest of Deuteronomy, and forward to Hebrews, which reads Joshua's rest as an unfulfilled shadow pointing to the rest that remains in Christ. The solidarity of the tribes anticipates the New Testament vision of one people who belong to and labor for one another.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Joshua taking up his commission in concrete action, to record the eastern tribes honoring their pledge so that all Israel enters the rest together, and to display a united people responding to God's word with wholehearted obedience and courage.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The text describes Israel's mobilization and the specific covenant arrangement with the eastern tribes; the death penalty for rebellion and the military crossing are part of that unique historical and covenantal moment, not commands for the church. What endures and instructs is the pattern of obedience to God's word, action grounded in His promise, and God's people refusing to rest in their own blessing while their brothers still lack.

debates.question: Was it right for the eastern tribes to settle east of the Jordan, outside the land promised to Abraham?

views.label: A legitimate arrangement honored by God
views.case: Moses granted the request under a binding condition, Numbers 32, and here Joshua and God's narrative treat their inheritance as valid; the land east of the Jordan was among the territories God gave Israel to possess, and the tribes faithfully keep their word by crossing to fight.

views.label: A concession that carried later risk
views.case: Their choice, driven partly by their large herds, placed them on the far side of the Jordan from the heart of the nation and its worship, and later chapters show the tension this could create, Joshua 22; some read it as a permitted but spiritually precarious decision.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage stresses their obligation to the whole: whatever the wisdom of settling east, they must not enjoy their rest while their brothers remain unsettled, a picture of shared responsibility among God's people.

study.originalLanguages: The section's key words carry its theology. Israel is commanded to go in and yarash, take possession of, the land, a verb that appears again and again in Joshua and holds two truths together: the land is purely God's gift, and yet it must be actively entered and taken hold of by faith and obedience. What God gives is menuchah, rest, not merely a cessation of fighting but a settled, secure home, a word that will echo all the way to Hebrews 4 and the Sabbath rest that remains for God's people. Each tribe's portion is its nachalah, its inheritance by promise; the eastern tribes already hold theirs, yet they must labor for their brothers' before returning to it. And at the end the people take the very word God had spoken to Joshua, chazaq, be strong, and speak it back to him, be strong and courageous. The vocabulary shows a people gathering God's promise, God's rest, and God's own charge into a single, united, obedient response.

typology.type: The settled tribes crossing over to win their brothers' rest before enjoying their own
typology.fulfillment: The solidarity of the body of Christ, where members bear one another's burdens and no one is meant to reach the inheritance alone, Galatians 6:2 and 1 Corinthians 12:26.

typology.type: The rest God gives His people in the land
typology.fulfillment: The rest Christ gives to all who come to Him, and the Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God, Matthew 11:28-29 and Hebrews 4:9-10.

typology.type: Joshua mobilizing one people for a single inheritance
typology.fulfillment: Christ gathering one people from many into one inheritance and one hope, Ephesians 4:4-6.

study.christ: The great word of this passage is rest, menuchah, the settled home God is giving His people. Yet Hebrews teaches that Israel's rest in the land was never the final rest; it was a signpost. So the Lord Jesus stands and says, Come to Me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest, Matthew 11:28. He is the greater Joshua who leads His people not merely across a river into a land they will lose, but through death into the rest that remains, Hebrews 4:8-10. And the solidarity commanded here, none resting while their brothers lack, is fulfilled in the church He forms, His body, where each member labors for the others until all together enter the inheritance He has secured.
study.god: God stands behind every command in this scene. The land is what the LORD your God is giving you, repeated so that no one mistakes conquest for self-achievement. The rest is His gift, promised through Moses and now near. He is the God who binds His people into one, so that the settled tribes cannot rest apart from their brothers, and He is the God whose word is worthy of the total obedience the people pledge, everything you have commanded us we will do. Even the courage the people speak is really a confession about God, that He was with Moses and will be with Joshua. The passage shows a God who gives, who unites, and whose faithfulness makes bold, obedient action the only fitting response.

crossReferences.note: The original pledge of Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh to Moses, to send armed men across the Jordan until Israel is settled, which Joshua now enforces.
Moses repeats the charge to the eastern tribes, using the language of rest that Joshua echoes here.
Jesus offers the rest that Israel's land-rest only foreshadowed, rest for the soul to all who come to Him.
Joshua did not give the people final rest; a Sabbath rest remains for the people of God in Christ.
Bear one another's burdens, the New Testament pattern of the shared labor the eastern tribes embody.

study.oneStory: God's charge to Joshua now becomes a whole nation on the move. Israel prepares to cross the Jordan, the settled tribes take up their brothers' cause so that all may share the rest, and the people answer with united obedience, echoing God's own word. It is a further step in the long story of God bringing His people into the inheritance He promised, and the rest they pursue points beyond the land to the lasting rest secured by the greater Joshua, Jesus.

quiz.q: What was Joshua's first command to the people as their new leader?

options.text: Prepare provisions, because within three days they would cross the Jordan to take the land.
options.note: Verses 10 to 11 record this as Joshua's opening order through the officers.

options.text: Assemble at the tabernacle to renew the covenant first.
options.note: Covenant renewal comes later in Joshua; his first command here is to prepare to cross within three days.
Spies are sent in chapter 2, but Joshua's first recorded command is to prepare provisions to cross.

options.text: Wait a full month for the Jordan floodwaters to fall.
options.note: He commands crossing within three days, while the river is at flood, not waiting for it to subside.

quiz.q: Why did Joshua remind the eastern tribes of their pledge?

options.text: Their fighting men must cross over to help until all the tribes had rest, before returning to their own land.
options.note: Verses 12 to 15 hold them to the pledge made to Moses in Numbers 32.

options.text: They were to give up their eastern land entirely and settle in Canaan.
options.note: They kept their eastern inheritance; the pledge was to fight for their brothers first, not to abandon their land.

options.text: They had to pay the other tribes for the land they held.
options.note: No payment is involved; the obligation was to share in the fighting until all had rest.

options.text: They were being punished for choosing land east of the Jordan.
options.note: This is not punishment; Joshua holds them to a promise they had freely made.

quiz.q: What principle governs the tribes' shared inheritance?

options.text: No tribe should enjoy its rest while other tribes still lack theirs.
options.note: The eastern tribes must help win their brothers' land before returning to their own, verses 14 to 15.

options.text: Each tribe should look after its own inheritance and leave the others to theirs.
options.note: The opposite: the settled tribes are bound to labor for the others, not to keep to themselves.

options.text: The strongest tribes deserve the largest share.
options.note: Allotment is by God's assignment, not strength; the point here is shared labor for a shared rest.

options.text: Only the tribes who crossed the Jordan matter to God.
options.note: The eastern tribes are fully included; the concern is that all Israel share the rest together.

quiz.q: How did the people respond to Joshua's leadership?

options.text: With wholehearted obedience, pledging to do all he commanded and echoing God's charge, be strong and courageous.
options.note: Verses 16 to 18 show their eager, united response and their echo of God's word to Joshua.

options.text: They obeyed but complained about the danger of the flooded Jordan.
options.note: They did obey, but the text records eager commitment, not complaint, unlike the wilderness generation.

options.text: They demanded that Joshua first prove himself in battle.
options.note: They asked for no proof; they pledged obedience and prayed God would be with him as with Moses.

options.text: They refused until Joshua reduced the workload.
options.note: There is no refusal; they promise full obedience, everything you have commanded us we will do.

questions: The settled tribes were called to fight for their brothers before enjoying their own rest. Where might God be calling you to labor for someone else's good rather than settling into your own comfort?
The people pledged, everything you have commanded us we will do, and everywhere you send us we will go. Is there an area where your obedience to God is partial rather than wholehearted?
Israel had to prepare and act on a promise that was still ahead of them. What promise of God are you being called to move toward in faith right now?
The rest Israel sought in the land points to the deeper rest Jesus gives. Where are you weary, and what would it mean to bring that weariness to Christ for His rest?
The people encouraged Joshua with the very words God had spoken to him. Who in your life needs you to speak God's word back to them as encouragement this week?

translationNotes.note: Mighty men of valor renders a Hebrew phrase for able-bodied warriors; the eastern tribes' soldiers, not their whole population, were to cross and fight.
Rest here renders menuchah, a settled resting place; the same theme of God-given rest runs from Deuteronomy through to Hebrews 4.
The penalty of death for rebellion reflects Israel's covenant law under Moses in a unique historical moment and is not a rule carried over to the church.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 1:10-18. Prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
